strTitle: Inquire about Visas for Galilean Confederacy
strTooltip: Browse and purchase visa application materials for Galilean Confederacy (Port Mojave).

strDesc:
Most sovereignties in the System observe a tiered immigration status, or "strata." Usually ranging from undocumented (illegal) immigrant, up to full citizenship, a person's strata dictates which rights they enjoy with the sovereignty.

Rights can include access to certain areas or services, eligibility for private housing or ship docking, enhanced prices on trade authorization, or even legal protections or permissions.

Such rights are usually conferred by parents through birth (jus sanguinis), and in some cases, by being born in the territory (jus soli).

Obtaining legal status after birth usually involves applying for a visa, and may include getting a sponsor and/or paying a significant fee.

Cost: $5,000,000

Galilean Confederacy's Platinum Candidate (PC) targets immigrant entrepreneurs with the skills and potential to build businesses in Port Mojave that are innovative, can create jobs within the Galilean Confederacy, and can compete on a System-wide scale.

Do you have an innovative business idea, and the means to grow your business? If you can prove your ability to contribute to the advancement of Galilean Confederacy, you may be able to immigrate with this visa class.

After submitting your application fee, your information is vetted through a cursory background check. Galilean Confederacy Immigration & Naturalization Authority (INA) could still discover a problem with your application during its full investigation, but at least the preliminary check passes.

Congratulations! You are now the proud holder of Permanent Resident status in the Galilean Confederacy!

Go enjoy the trade discounts and private housing opportunities.

Cost: $750,000

The Skilled Laborer (SL) program is one of Galilean Confederacy's Platinum Development Board (PDB) systems for processing immigration applications. The SL program allows candidates with foreign work experience and no connections to Galilean Confederacy to apply for permanent residency.

In order to qualify for SL, a candidate must have work experience in one of the following in-demand skills:

-Excavation
-Civil Engineering
-Electrical Engineering

Those meeting the skill requirements with sufficient funds have an option to purchase this status.

strTitle: Pay for Skilled Labor GalCon visa
strDesc:
After submitting your application fee, your information is vetted through a cursory background check. Galilean Confederacy Immigration & Naturalization Authority (INA) assesses your proficiency in the desired skillsets with a battery of tests.

Congratulations! You are now the proud holder of Permanent Resident status in the Galilean Confederacy!

Go enjoy the trade discounts and private housing opportunities.

Cost: $250,000

Immigrants can also fast-track their application through the Draft Service (DS) track. By materially contributing to the war effort, whether that be through mining, beacon-laying, or destruction of enemy materials, applicants can raise their clearance levels to become eligible as a Serviceman.

Those meeting the clearance requirements with sufficient funds have an option to apply for this status.

After submitting your application fee, a background check, and the a review of your material contributions, you are deemed an acceptable profile for admittance to DS.

Congratulations! You are now the proud holder of Permanent Resident status in the Galilean Confederacy!

Go enjoy the trade discounts and private housing opportunities.

Cost: $500,000

The Heritage Sponsorship Program (HSP) in Galilean Confederacy is a component of Galilean Confederacy's immigration system, enabling Galilean Confederacy citizens and permanent residents to sponsor their spouses, family, or trusted friends for permanent residency in the Galilean Confederacy. The program aims to facilitate family reunification by allowing Galilean Confederacy citizens or permanent residents to bring their loved ones to live with them.

(Requires a Galilean Confederacy resident to agree to sponsor applicant first.)

After submitting your application fee, a background check, and the requisite interview of your sponsor, you are deemed an acceptable profile for admittance to HSP.

Congratulations! You are now the proud holder of Permanent Resident status in the Galilean Confederacy!

Go enjoy the trade discounts and private housing opportunities.

Visa application services are only relevant to those who do not already have legal status in the Galilean Confederacy.

As a citizen of the Galilean Confederacy, you do not need this service.

The Pacific Airlines Space Service shuttle is arriving...

You step board the shuttle, and scan the room for any available seats to strap into.

It's a smallish vessel, and mostly a mix of folks not looking like they're about to step into the void to spin some wrenches. Indies tend to have their own ship, while day laborers use whatever barge their foreman provides that shift.

So what's left here are those crossing the cold void to see a distant friendly face, or attend an on-site. Or maybe they're on the first leg of a longer voyage. The kind of folk who brought some leftovers to eat en route, or a book on their PDA.

You strap-in to avoid delaying them any longer, and the rapid hissing of RCS signals your departure.
The shuttle arrives at your destination.

You turn away the PASS vessel, and remain where you are.

Pacific Airlines Space Service policy requires passengers to pay the full voyage fare if PASS vessel service is cancelled at point of boarding.

Customers may cancel before vessel arrival for a nominal cancellation fee.

Pacific Airlines Space Service policy prohibits passengers who are recently involved in combat from boarding PASS vessels.

Customers are advised to call again for ferry service when all conflicts have been resolved.

The clamps on your docking ring engage and your hull resonates like a struck gong. While your sensors scan the vessel for data, you close your eyes and imagine the fortune that could await you on the other side.

Perhaps you've found a racing skiff: a nimble vessel with a lightweight hull pushed around by a top-of-the-line RCS package and a four can Miura "Hydra" Intake Regulator. Or maybe you've stumbled on a multi-cabin pleasure yacht: an elite catamaran complete with a full bar and corridors adorned with yards of crimson and cream Van Hummel interiors.

Or maybe it's just a dead freighter. A decades-old Testudo hauler packed with trash and sporting a giant hole where the reactor used to be. This is a junkyard, after all.

Whatever it is, you've got access to it now, and a bit of time before Ayosec security goons harrass you.

If you're a salvager, maybe you'll smash and grab rare parts to fence back at OKLG? Cut out whatever you can quickly for a buck. Or with a license, trade a bit of profit for less pressure and more resale reliability.

If you're a mechanic, you could fix it up. Make it fly. Derelicts go for a discount at the ship broker, and any sweat equity you put into it after you buy it is yours to keep. You could even resell it when you're done for a tidy profit.

Or, if you're an outlaw, you could always just steal it. Sometimes it can feel pretty good to stay one step ahead of Ayotimiwa's corporate security goons, if you're willing to give up docking it at patrolled ports.

From the outside, the Flotilla looks like an impossible place: a hodgepodge of dead ships welded together at odd angles, entire hulls recognizable and intact, as if a dark star collapsed and sucked half the Boneyard into a single doomed point.

Inside it's a twisting warren of corridors leading to cabins of varying, vague, and ever shifting purpose. Trash and shadows gather in every corner. Figures dart and huddle and lie in heaps. A distinct melange of odors lend the place an air of the tawdry exotic: food, cigarettes, ozone, urine, perfume.

Apparently it all started with a humble salvage pod and a pissed off spacer. Accounts differ as to whether she was dodging criminals or law enforcement or simple docking fees, but in any case, she flew her little Ryokka TU-77a about three thousand clicks outside of 1036 Ganymed, welded it to the side of a reactor-powered Testudo Myna, and parked there for good.

Her tantrum wouldn't have mattered if it weren't for a clever bit of engineering. She managed to hack together a controller for her RCS package that perfectly mimicked the oblong, years-long orbit of Ganymed. Soon after that, a heavy hitter in the criminal underworld caught wind of a spot outside the prying eyes of Ayotimiwa Security, moved his outfit onboard, and the rest is history. Sordid, bloody, vile history.

Now, years on, the Flotilla has ripened into a refuge for criminals, thieves, freedom fighters, the insane. It's become a catch-all for anyone who refuses to hack it in the dead-end labor grind Ayotimiwa sponsors on OKLG station. There are no cops. No docking fees. A scrap trade for hull parts keeps the economy humming, along with a weapons broker, various drug dealers, and the odd humanitarian.

Still, despite all this, there's something to admire about the Flotilla, and the desperate people aboard it. Unlike OKLG, where everyone has their hand in each other's pocket, the spacers here are bound by something stronger than a contract.

The simple fact is that they share needs that can only be met by relying on one another. The need to eat, the need to sleep, the need to breathe. There's a certain pride to living here, an enduring knowledge that despite the fact that you might get stabbed in the liver, at least you won't die wearing an Ayotimiwa t-shirt.

A rush of gas escapes the bridge as you force the door open.

Once upon a time this hot little number ferried Panmen to Qiantangmen, Mars L4 to L5 and back, forty-eight people a pop, all the while rated for just thirty-two. A whole fleet of 'em just like it ran that relay on gas blasts so fine and cheap they were hardly there at all.

But in time, the operation evolves or dies. Mars is coming up in the worlds. Their workhorses turn into old nags, are sent out to pasture, here, in K-LEG, awaiting the tender mercies of a full shipbreaker's crew in an Ayo main bay.

K-LEG receives one ship a week, every week, from anywhere and everywhere in the system. It has for almost fifty years. With a full head of steam, Ayotimiwa can just about process one ship a week. Following exigencies of labor and best laid plans, there are now hundreds of ships in a backlog so long and convoluted that they would never see another human soul, let alone the inside of the breaker's yard.

That's where you come in.
Player entered the hidden zone to trigger the hallway tutorial.

For those who have spent their lives in the hollow, darkened corridors of spacecraft far from the warmth of Sol, the Newcal aerostat presents itself as a paradise.

Cosmopolitan crowds meander through a cavernous space warmed by sunlight passing through a seemingly endless stretch of transparent walls and ceilings. Beyond the glass, a stunning array of clouds and caustic atmospheric gasses flash and gather in a shifting ombre of blue, pink, and orange. The internal atmosphere, perfectly calibrated to maximize both human comfort and aerostat buoyancy, encircles you like a warm, dry sheet, and carries within it a subtle aroma of flowers and herbs.

Commissioned by the United States after a series of experimental aerostats failed in design, Newcal was finally finished in 2049, and is the only Venusian aerostat system large enough to be considered a major metropolitan area. While originally a US colony, Newcal officially declared its independence from the Old United States in 2059, after the NCN Stevie Nicks was mistakenly shot down by automated defenses in Earth Low Orbit. The acting government of Newcal continues to claim that at the time of the incident the US command structure was compromised.

The city consists of several modular aerostat regions, including among others a high-end shopping district called San Diego and a new advanced aerostat called Los Angeles, currently under construction and slated to be complete in 2083. Since gaining independence Newcal has advanced into a robust parallel information and service economy driven primarily by influencers seeking corporate sponsorships by visiting shops and restaurants where they advocate their patrons on the social media platform Rin Ren.

The Los Angeles construction district greets you with rebar, tools, catwalks, and sulfur-tinged haze. LA is the NewCal's newest district awaiting completion and integration into the city aerostat proper.

Of course, as with every corporate project, construction is behind schedule and over budget.

Progress is also hindered by jealous factions from Encantado and Cloudbreak who pay lucratively for illicit deliveries of the new construction tech. They justify the sponsorship of a booming sabatoge trade by claiming the tech was all developed from data stolen from the older aerostats in the first place.

With a discarded construction tech ID and a fritzy wall panel, you manage to override the Los Angeles Construction Zone service dock, granting you future direct ship access via comms uplink.

NewCal PD and Air Navy may not like it, but you can now dock directly for easier access and exfiltration.

Дверь станции K-Leg открывается, и вас встречает знакомый аромат блюд, которые продают на станции: жареные креветки в соусе суя карри, кебаб из курицы с пряностями яджи, говядина по-сычуаньски с розовым перцем. Ваш разум понимает, что мясо выращено в аквапонике, в лаборатории или, что еще хуже, но ваше сердце подсказывает вам, что все, приготовленное руками человека, лучше, чем еда из Тренчерса.

Уставшие космонавты ходят между койками, заправочным киоском и станцией технического обслуживания, проходя сквозь паутину сигаретного дыма, которая постоянно висит над станцией. Курят Sungara Viceroys, любимую марку на свалке. Здешние люди курят их без фильтра, сколько бы раз ни штрафовали их руководители Ayotimiwa за перегрузку скрубберов CO2.

Ваш новый корабль ждет в порту, недалеко от киосков и лавок по продаже металлолома. Как только вы его найдете, сможете наконец отстыковаться от этой скалы. Если вы хотите разобрать корабли на запчасти, вам понадобятся только ваши ручные инструменты. Но для ремонта и переделки корпусов вам понадобится сварочный аппарат.

В любом случае, если вам повезет, вы наконец-то сможете зарабатывать на жизнь. Что-то большее, чем корпоративная гонка за ежедневной зарплатой, разбивая корабли. Жизнь, которую вы можете назвать своей.

Stepping off the tram at K-Leg's commercial district, the difference is immediately apparent. It's not nicer, but...maybe less worn-out? Like less people come here. Or at least less rough and tumble types.

The main point of interest here is the Port Authority office. A place every captain inevitably becomes familiar with, as they navigate the web of red tape surrounding spaceport operations.

Usually, you'll find at least a few pilots and ship owners slouched in line here, down on their luck, and hoping for a break.

The longer the line, the more likely that the "nice" clerk is on duty today.

strTitle: Ship Broker, Medical Kiosk, and Colony Embassies
strDesc: This district also offers spacers with funds to spare plenty of lavish means to part with their windfalls. The Ship Broker offers mortgages on pre-owned vessels as well as derelicts from the Boneyard stock. The Van Buren Self-Care kiosk offers medical products and treatments for acute and chronic ailments. Embassies for distant colonies allow application for permanent resident visas, coveted for tax discounts and improved legal treatment. Unfortunately, the decade since the loss of Earth to the ablative cascade of '59 has rendered colony interaction increasingly rare and informal, and legal status highly coveted, let alone full citizenship. Legal status often confers local benefits such as cargo trade discounts and rights to purchase private housing and docking.
strTooltip: Learn about Ship Broker, Medical Kiosk, and Colony Embassies

strDesc:
The red neons cast heavy shadows in the Old Emporium, K-Leg's only leisure district. The OE is a relic of a former world, a portal to the halcyon days of the wildcat Nigerian colony before Earth's ablation cascade gutted the economy. Spacers come here to escape the bureaucratic grind, to imagine a world without bosses, to split a chilled bottle of Bismertnaya even when they can't afford to.

A dozen meters away from the shuttle stop you see a knot of revelers gathered in the warm light of the Mescaform bar. Laughter and conversation float through Mescaform in a melange of English, Mandarin, and even Yoruba. That last one might be a sign Ayo managers are mixing it up with the common folk.

Whatever the language, the warm organics of human voices in close proximity is a welcome departure from the hollow echo of comm chatter.

One of the faces in the crowd is The Fixer. They're a known quantity in the station community, K-Leg's portal to a system-wide, ever shifting Black Market whose appetite for used ship parts and other rare salvage can never be sated.

Shouldn't be too hard to get The Fixer talking. Just mention you have something to trade and they're likely to be interested.

Calico City welcomes you, as long as you don't intend to linger.

Ceres persists on a careful diet of mining, independence, and vigilante justice. The population here is ambitious, clamoring, and uneasy.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to the Mint! But don't call it that on-board or you may find yourself inconvenienced by a nicked carotid artery. The CCRE claims the days of piracy are over, but it also absolves itself of responsibility for the manner in which residents or mercs may decide to preserve the station's dignity.

The 'Ring Station' runs on respect, offered and earned, while operating the most lucrative mining processing industry in the System. Belt colonies and corporations send ore, and the Ring Station spins it into gold. The population here is ambitious, clamoring, and uneasy.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

You step on to the strange vessel, wondering what it could possibly be doing so far away from OKLG. Looking around you realize that, much like a decaying relationship, the ship was clearly built with love but is now in dire need of TLC. You recognize the bones of a Testudo Heavy Tug, but it's been heavily modified. Several coats of a cloying yellow-gold have been applied to all the floors and walls, some more recently than others. The ground is littered with cigarette butts, rocky debris, and half-eaten Nacho Fiesta Trenchers. Down the central corridor you hear a percussive banging that serves as the backbeat to an a capella melody belted at enthusiastic volume. You strain to make out the words: "Fire on the mountain! *BANG* "Static in the black!" *BANG.* "That bright seam calls, so I obey, and I'm never goin back!" *BANG BANG!* You pause for a moment and consider the wisdom of interrupting someone so deeply involved in their flow. The song does call to you in a spirit of optimism, however. It's hard not to be charmed by it. "Folks'll call you crazy." *BANG* "But they ain't glimpsed the gold!" *BANG*. "I'll tap the vein that buys me Mars and that's where I'll grow old!" *BANG BANG*

Welcome to Jade Rabbit! First home of humanity's off-world endeavors, and Xinhua's stepping-stone to space supremacy. Now the crumbling art deco facades conceal a collapsed and possibly haunted tourism bubble that refuses to die.

The squatters who call Jade Rabbit home scrape an economy out of He-3 production, reliving long-past glory days, and specious claims of intermittent communications relayed from Old Earth. The population here is hungry, clamoring, and uneasy.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

You shouldn't be here. The old world died in 2059, as the Kessler Syndrome lit the sky.

While the ablation cascade wreaked havok on humanity off-world, those remaining on the surface have suffered worse. The population here is ambitious, clamoring, and uneasy. Pragmatic scavengers proclaiming a New Earth Ostracon holds answers about what is lost, and what is to come.

Welcome to Qincheng Station. If you have business with Mercury and Prokofiev Penal Colony, conduct it at Qincheng. If not, be on your way.

Qincheng's existence relies on thriving via stability and maintaining an unruffled status quo, even in the face of scarcity, aging infrastructure, and ever more one-way immigrants bound for the surface. The population here is ambitious, clamoring, and uneasy.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to Atlantis! The submarine colony nestled self-sufficiently under the ice crusts of Europa.

Atlantis supplies the lion's share of the System's water trade, along with research coveted by Voltaire and neutral diplomatic positioning pursued by both the Galilean Confederacy and Titan. The population here is patient and ambitious, plying Europa's mysterious, clamoring, and uneasy oceans.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome one and all to Fort Simpson! The capital of the brand new capital of the Galilean Confederacy.

Where other colonies have resources, administrative acumen, and earned diplomatic clout, Fort Simpson thrives in spirit and eager optimism. The population here is eager, clamoring, and uneasy.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to Porto Nuevo Station! The gateway to the Jovian industrial colonies, the many moons and Trojans.

Jupiter originally dominated mineral and gas mining, before the Galilean Confederacy threw the Jovian colonies into revolution against corporate rule. The population here is hard-working, clamoring, and uneasy.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to Hanzhou! Humanities' finest and grandest achievement. Decades in the making. The first and likely the only Earth-like paradise among the stars.

Hanzhou Orbital offers health and leisure available nowhere else in the System, but as to be expected, immigration is therefore formidable and unflinching.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to 'The Lab'. If you're looking for anything good, you're in the wrong place. Formerly the overwhelmed Noctis Regional Processing Facility, now a miserable gathering place of humanity unwelcome elsewhere.

Not much catches the eye here, and much offends it.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to Suzhou! While still impressive, the underfunded Suzhou Orbital will always pale in comparison to the paradise of its sister orbital Hangzhou.

Suzhou Orbital primarily leases acres and acres of its internal surface area to corporations seeking minimal regulation and oversight such as DND Cola, Renbao Smart Technologies, Inc., and Ogiso's Resgister.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Tharsis Landing welcomes all. The capital of Xinhua culture and the the most populous center of trade and influence System-wide.

You'll find nearly anything money can buy in Tharsis Landing, as well as everything else that comes along with making such dreams possible.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to Voltaire! Home to Brave New World Corporation's famous Voltaire University and high-tech research facility par excellence.

Voltaire hosts the System's best biomedical labs, and is always seeking science materials (and test subjects) to fuel its production of ever new marvels of modern technology.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to Virginia! The capital and crowning jewel of American space expansion efforts, forever shining in Saturn's breath-taking orbit.

Virginia thrives on military tech and the most sophisticated and vast shipbuilding operation extant in the System after the ablation cascade severed Old Earth. The population here is ambitious, clamoring, and uneasy.

Perhaps in time, visitors will be welcome to participate in more than simply port and trade activities.

Welcome to Cloudbreak aerostat! The smallest Venusian aerostat, the active science station Cloudbreak is rather unremarkable compared with its neighbors.

Cloudbreak's population is minimal, and most residents are studious sorts, collaborating to research and develop new sensor technology while studying Venus' atmosphere, climate, and surface.

It's still Venus, so there are plenty of influencers, documentarists, and drama-mongers waiting for Cloudbreak to have its day. One such possibility is brewing factionalism pitting Cloudbreak and Encantado against the more powerful Newcal.

Cloudbreak welcomes any aerostat construction tech you're willing to sell.

Welcome to Encantado aerostat! The oldest aerostat on the planet, famous for its cloud racing leagues, Grande Prêmio do Encantado, and, of course, gambling. Visit Sol Vermelho Lounge if you want in on the racing action yourself, or if you simply want to be seen among the influencers, celebrities, and System-famous entertainers that tend to gather here.

The aging aerostat is far less impressive than sunny Newcal, but that just means folks here pay handsomely for aerostat tech and salvage nicked from Newcal's construction project, a new Los Angeles district.

Welcome to the Venus Orbital Station! While certainly less culturally and economically significant than the Venusian aerostat colonies, VORB thrives as a trade and refueling hub for spacefaring vessels incapable of either entering Venus' thick and volatile atmosphere, or exiting Venus' gravity well.

During long shifts spent eviscerating half-dead ships in the Boneyard, you often wondered what it would feel like to do the opposite. Why wrench out the beating heart from a wounded ship when you could just nurse it back to health instead?

The question returns to you as you look around this derelict, now through the eyes of a self-employed spacer. The prospect of finding an answer has you feeling like an uncaged animal. The sound of your breathing repeats in your helmet and grows more eager with every exhalation.

You set to work and your tools resound through the hull all around you. During idle moments it's hard not to consider the consequences of installing and uninstalling parts you don't own. Sure, you're in a junkyard. But it's Ayotimiwa's junkyard. And while they might turn a blind eye to the salvage trade when it suits them, they will bring the hammer down as soon as it doesn't.

That goes double if you happen to make a derelict fly. The standard blocks on a Nav Station are onerous, but far from prohibitive. And clever engineering can take you a long way.

But as soon as trash becomes treasure, you can be certain corporation security won't be far behind. And, if you're not careful, possibly a few years in the brig.

Of course, one upside to Ayo being fueled by blood and profit is that they'll sell anything to anyone for the right price. The Ship Broker Kiosk in the OKLG Commercial district keeps a record of every hunk of junk out here. Including this one. So that's where you'd go if you decided to purchase her proper.

Either way, stealing or buying, it won't be cheap. But freedom never is.
[us] views info on [them].
strTooltip: Displays information about registering for and viewing racing leagues

strDesc: This kiosk allows you to join leagues and view standings and results of most recent races. Racers complete each race in a league individually and in order. Times are compared for league standings. To start a race: 1. First join a league from the racing kiosk (paying the entry fee). Races proceed in order for that league. Your available options depend on your demonstrated ability. 2. While in a league, use your Comms options from your Nav Station to hail the race's kiosk station (e.g. VENC) and select "RACING". 3. Select either "Start practice" or "Start race" from the Comms option to begin the league's next race. Practice as much as you like, but each race occurs only once per league. The timer does not begin immediately. 4. Fly to the racing waypoints. Pass the Start gate to start your time. 5. Fly between paired gate markers (i.e. 1A and 1B) to progress in the race. Usually gates must be passed in numeric order, but some tracks differ. Earn points by completing races, and racers with the highest points in a league earn prize money.

PointToPoint A rally track. Go from the Start gate, pass through gates in order and end at the last gate. Circuit A full-circuit lap track. Go from the Start gate, through gates in order and end at the start gate (possibly with more than 1 lap) Orientation A freestyle navigation track. Go from the Start gate through all the gates in any order you wish and end by passing through the start gate.

As comfortable as it feels to return to the station after scouring the cold boneyard for parts, in the world of semi-legal salvage, finding parts is only half the battle. The rest is selling it.

Station centers are connected by rail, a single car shuttle system that is constantly in a state of disrepair. With a working knowledge of the station and an appetite for risk, you might even find a way to make a living on this rock.

The OKLG economy is a strange beast. There's the corporate sponsored salvage trade, run through the scrap and license kiosks. With the right license you can rent time out in the boneyard and pick parts without being bothered by the AyoSec goons.

The black market is another matter. Industrious scavengers know that if you want a premium price for a rare piece, then you're going to have to find a reliable fence. But it won't be easy gaining their trust.

Lastly, the GigNexus shops out jobs to just about anyone. The work ranges from menial station jobs to high-risk boarding operations. In every case, the faster you work, the more money you'll find.

A slight pang of humiliation rushes through you, as the audible buzzer announces your permission is denied. Unpaid docking fees.

"I'm sure it happens all the time," you assure yourself. Surely they won't assume you were trying to skip town without paying?

In any case, you'll need to pay those fees before you're going anywhere. Either click your money total for the Finances ledger, or use one of the ubiquitous orange station kiosks.

That, or find someone at the Port Authority desk who might just let you slide this one time...

Fourth shift ends and the change in the atmosphere is unmistakable. Shipbreakers stop sparking their hand torches. Long-armed construction waldos drop their grip and drift away from wrecked hulls. Two clicks out, a cargo hauler cuts its reactor and the entire rig burns down like a cold ember in the dark.

Untime: the closest anyone out here will ever get to a Sabbath. Twenty minutes and fifty-eight glorious seconds welded to the end of shift four, a seemingly insignificant stretch of time remaining like loose change after the rest of the hours are divided, counted and spent.

In the beginning, Untime was merely a quirk of the New Celestial Calendar, a more even way to divide the year now that Humanity had uncoupled itself from Earth and its overwrought lunar calendar. But that was decades ago. These days, Untime is for rest, for conversation, for anything at all that won't turn a buck for the bosses.

As for the managers who choose not to observe this fine tradition? Two weeks ago one of them "fell" from a labor barge and hit every ship on the way down.

With one last look back at the barracks you've called home all these years, you step through the threshold into your salvage pod. It won't get far on its own, but you know of at least a few derelicts, out there in the K-Leg boneyard, that might be candidates for your next ship.

Start checking the derelicts out, and choose one to make your own.

With one last look back at the barracks you've called home all these years, you step through the threshold into your salvage pod. It won't get far on its own, but you know of at least a few derelicts, out there in the K-Leg boneyard, that might be candidates for your next ship.

Start checking the derelicts out, and choose one to make your own.

The call comes through the tinny speakers. "This is Utopia Risk Management," says a bored voice on the other side of the airlock. "We are legally boarding you under section 27-2 of the Ayotimiwa Operational License Agreement. Have one of your crew report to the airlock and stand well back with your personal devices set to I.D. display."

strTitle: The Purple Jumpsuit of The Mariner
strDesc:
People can't stop staring. You walk into a room and the chatter dies down as all eyes are drawn to your body sheathed in radiant periwinkle. It's as if you were The Mariner herself.

It's hard to say where the superstitions about The Mariner and the color purple began. It could be as simple as a last name. Before she was The Mariner, the first person to be murdered in space was Olivia Violet Highsmith. She was by all accounts a cheerful NASA engineer hailing from rural Michigan. It's possible that after her infamous and violent passing, someone clever latched on to that middle name and never let go.

But that doesn't explain what The Mariner is said to look like. It doesn't explain the lavender haze that pumps from the severed O2 tube trailing her body. Nor does it describe the attraction. Mariner Seekers insist, and insist they can prove, that purple increases the likelihood of meeting the ancient specter by tenfold.

Doesn't matter now. The belief has stuck and your lavender jumpsuit proves it. The suit came from a merchandising gimmick sponsored by Damask Rose as part of the marketing campaign for their "Purple Curse" strain. That is, until a series of accidents befell customers wearing them, fueling rumors that Damask had angered the ghost by being so cavalier about her passing.

So what you've got on your hands is a collectors item. Just don't expect anyone to buy you any drinks.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of the Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said
strDesc:
It arrives without warning: a memory from long ago so embarrassing, so superheated by shame, that it feels like molten metal poured down the center of your back. 

Why did you say that? The question haunts you. You could have said anything, quite literally, and instead you said … that. You see their face, their eyes, their pained wince.

Your rational mind assures you that they've forgotten by now. Surely they've lost track of that catastrophically stupid thing you said. But the soul is not rational, and memory is not fair, so here it sits: The Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said, the moment as fresh now, years later, as it has ever been.

Wow. It's going to take a couple hours to shake that one off.

strTitle: Process the Memory of the Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said
strDesc: You do the difficult work of processing the harsh memory. While it causes you immediate emotional pain, it moves you closer to resolving the memory and its attendant traumas permanently. This kind of work, you're certain, will eventually yield deep emotional rewards.

strTitle: Revisited by the Memory of the Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said
strDesc: The difficult memory returns to you again uninvited. You can choose to suppress it, gaining a momentary respite from the fear it generates. Or you can do the difficult work of processing it, stirring up hard emotions in the present with the hope it might lead to permanent growth in the future.

strTitle: Process the Revisitation of the Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said
strDesc: You do the difficult work of processing the harsh memory. While it causes you immediate emotional pain, it moves you closer to resolving the memory and its attendant traumas permanently. This kind of work, you're certain, will eventually yield deep emotional rewards.

strTitle: Final Memory of the Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said

Or you can do the difficult work of processing it, stirring up hard emotions in the present with the hope it might lead to permanent growth in the future.

strTitle: Resolve Your Memory of the Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said
strDesc: After a long internal struggle, you've managed to work through this particular harsh memory and its trauma. Having done so, you've grown deeply as a person, and gained a valuable trait. It will serve you well in the future.

strTitle: Suppress Memory of The Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said
strDesc:
You suppress this memory in the hopes it will go away for a while.

You find a temporary peace in this, even a mild boost to your mood.

Who needs introspection, after all?

strTitle: Suppress Memory of The Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said Again
strDesc:
You once again suppress this memory, knowing it will give a boost of mood in the present.

It doesn't bring you as much peace as before to flee from this memory, but it still feels safer than processing such an ugly moment.

Surely you can do this forever. Right?

strTitle: Suppress Memory of The Dumbest Thing You've Ever Said Yet Again
strDesc:
For the third time you suppress this memory, knowing you'll escape it for a while. But after all this running from your past, you no longer feel a sense of peace in flight.

In fact, dodging the memory has changed you, perhaps irrevocably.

There's a lesson here, if you choose to see it.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of a Love Now Lost
strDesc:
You find yourself mulling over their memory again, stretching it out like an aching muscle, returning to a pain you've grown fond of because it is, at the very least, familiar.

It was one of your shortest relationships. Perhaps for that very reason it is the one that endures, the one you miss in the quiet moments.

It's the sense memories that remain, mostly. The sound of their laugh. The softness of their forearm felt beneath your thumb. The scent of them between the ear and the nape of the neck.

It's better to have loved and lost, they say. And in moments like this, when you're adrift and otherwise alone in the cold eternity of space, you believe it to be true.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of Foliage
strDesc:
An image of green flora, real flora, arrives to you from your past without warning. The plant was slick and glistening with dew, bedded in rich black soil, and home to more darting life in the form of gnats, mosquitos, and tiny spiders whose nearly invisible webs made them appear as if they were frozen in midair.

Merely a generation ago such a sight would mean almost nothing. One image among thousands collected during the course of normal living. But here, in the cold dead reaches of space, where the monotony of black nothing is broken only steel and plastic, the memory is as freighted with loss as it is enchanted by promise.

You find yourself wondering, quite earnestly, if you'll ever see such a thing again. Yes, there are places in The System where things still grow. But now that Earth is nothing but a broken promise, these places exist in an income bracket so far beyond your own that they are almost unheard of.

But they remain imaginable. And if there's one thing the corps can't kill, it is your spirit. So here you sit, imagining.

You do the difficult work of processing the harsh memory. While it causes you immediate emotional pain, it moves you closer to resolving the memory and its attendant traumas permanently. This kind of work, you're certain, will eventually yield deep emotional rewards.

strTitle: Revisited by the Memory of Foliage
strDesc: The difficult memory returns to you again uninvited. You can choose to suppress it, gaining a momentary respite from the fear it generates. Or you can do the difficult work of processing it, stirring up hard emotions in the present with the hope it might lead to permanent growth in the future.

strDesc: You do the difficult work of processing the harsh memory. While it causes you immediate emotional pain, it moves you closer to resolving the memory and its attendant traumas permanently. This kind of work, you're certain, will eventually yield deep emotional rewards.

After a long internal struggle, you've managed to work through this particular harsh memory and its trauma. Having done so, you've grown deeply as a person, and gained a valuable trait. It will serve you well in the future.

You suppress this memory in the hopes it will go away for a while.

You find a temporary peace in this, even a mild boost to your mood.

Who needs introspection, after all?

You once again suppress this memory, knowing it will give a boost of mood in the present.

It doesn't bring you as much peace as before to flee from this memory, but it still feels safer than processing such an ugly moment.

Surely you can do this forever. Right?

strTitle: Suppress Memory of Foliage Yet Again
strDesc:
For the third time you suppress this memory, knowing you'll escape it for a while. But after all this running from your past, you no longer feel a sense of peace in flight.

In fact, dodging the memory has changed you, perhaps irrevocably.

There's a lesson here, if you choose to see it.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of a Friend who Passed
strDesc:
They say that grief is like a stone in your pocket. But now, as the memory of your dead friend comes rushing back, it feels more like a millstone around your neck.

When you were together it was easy. Your wavelengths were fully aligned. There was no need for performance or defense or flights away from conversation in search of privacy. A vid stream of nearly nothing could occupy you for hours because of the shared jokes and criticisms you'd develop. The creek trickle of your shallow chats would flow to the deep waters of meaningful conversation so naturally that you'd miss the point at which one became the next.

And now what? You're left with only their defunct RinRin profile as a reminder. And you've watched the vids on there so many times that they have started to feel like scenes from a documentary about a stranger.

Standing here now, in this moment, you're left wondering: can such a thing ever be recuperated? Replaced? And with cold certainty you realize that it cannot.

strTitle: Process the Memory of Friend Who Passed
strDesc:
For the first time you let the certainty of your loss descend upon you. You watch as all your denials, all your angers, all your bitter negotiations fall away like autumn leaves, leaving behind only the barren tree of your loneliness.

What you long for now, in this moment, is not love or meaning or even joy. It is the simple presence of someone you cared for. It is the reminder that the system is not merely a complex hallucination of a deranged mind, but instead a world with others in it, others not so different than yourself.

This newfound certainty that your friend is never coming back is a pain you can hardly bear. But it is the kind of pain that accompanies growth. And in the depths of your mind you know that, with enough time, it is a growth you will be grateful for.

strTitle: Revisited by the Memory of a Friend Who Passed
strDesc:
The truth of your loss returns to you suddenly. You see it again: the barren tree of grief, once lush with foliage, now stark and grim. On the horizon behind, you see dark clouds flickering white with lightning, as if the circuitry powering them is overloaded and failing.

You've already determined that this loss is permanent. And you've resolved yourself not to deny such a powerful truth. But the pain of being alone is still there, and judging by those clouds it will get worse before it gets better. What will you do with it?

strTitle: Process the Revisitation of Your Memory of a Friend Who Passed
strDesc:
You stand staring at the tree as the clouds approach and gather. Somehow the rain arrives before the clouds are even overhead, long ropes of it lashing at the ground and branches, each one snapping up clouds of dust that soon settle into thick black mud at your feet.

Your animal mind screams for relief as you're pounded with the reality of your loss. How could your friend leave you here, all alone in such a cruel and uncaring world? In your mind's eye you see shelter: a cave, a hovel really. You know that if you're willing to scrabble through the mud it is a place you could go to escape the storm.

But you don't. Somehow, through all this, you've gathered enough wisdom to know that there is no hiding from it. So you stand and let the rain lash. You watch the dark clouds as they shout and flash.

And, before long, you see it: the sun. Just there, behind the havoc of the storm's tantrum. If you had fled to the cave, you never would have found it.

strTitle: Final Memory of a Friend Who Passed
strDesc:
There it is again: the grief, the proverbial stone in your pocket. You run your fingers along the sharp edges, trace them to where they grow curved and soft.

Last time you were here it was a storm of sadness you just barely survived. You know you could go there again, provided you're strong enough to weather it. What will you do?

strTitle: Resolve Your Memory of a Friend Who Passed
strDesc:
When humanity flung itself from Earth just a few generations ago, the gesture was powered by a force of optimism that once free from our home we might expand the species beyond our basest needs.

But now, standing at the tree of your grief, staring at the sun as it burns the rain away into a soft mist that climbs into the sky above you, you finally understand that can never be true.

The need to eat. The need to sleep. And our deepest need, the need to be close to another with their own needs; these are grasping desires we can never, should never, fully shed.

Your friend is gone now, lifted into the mist of the next, if there is such a place. But the force of your desire to be with them again, once an impossible pain, feels now like an upwards tug at the heart, a gentle squeeze of your empty hand. It is a reminder: once you have been loved, you can never truly be alone.

You've managed with long internal struggle to fully process your memory of your friend's death. Through this, you've gained a new and rare sense of patience. It will serve you well in the future.

strDesc:
You suppress this memory in the hopes it will go away for a while.

You find a temporary peace in this, even a mild boost to your mood.

Who needs introspection, after all?

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of The Project You'll Never Finish
strDesc:
Later. Soonish. You'll get to it tomorrow, you tell yourself, when things slow down a little. When life stops happening to you and suddenly decides, for some reason you can't even imagine, to start respecting your to-do list.

Meanwhile your Big Project molders like a gargantuan river rock, wholly indifferent to the rushing currents of time splashing at its base. Every day of neglect creates new projects, new problems grown up from ignoring the first, each a creeping ivy, the mess so thick now that the initial rock is hardly visible.

You've lost track of the number of promises you've broken with yourself. At this point, you don't even have the energy to make another.

Now, in a moment of clarity borne on a wave of self disdain, you start to wonder if you're just not fit for the job.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of Someone You Betrayed
strDesc:
At the time it felt so easy. Like seeing an open door and merely walking through it.

It took just five or six words. Flimsy little things on their own, words. But when bolstered by the right facial expression, a wry little smile you dug deep to find, the sentiment was suddenly ironclad: "Hey. Would I lie to you?"

But lie you did. And down your friend went, down and down a darkened path you laid that led to their certain ruin.

And the worst part is? They still don't know it was you who sold them down the river. Poor thing.

No, that's not the worst part. The worst part is that standing here, as the memory returns, you know that you're capable of doing it all again.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of The Masses
strDesc:
You're reminded, suddenly, of being trapped among bodies. Dozens, hundreds of them, all fleeing a single source of danger, or supposed danger, as the inciting incident happened outside your sightline and became irrelevant the instant you were swept up in the stampede.

What mattered in the moment is that you were stuck, forced, kettled by wild-eyed LEO thugs barking about how non-lethal rounds will still ruin your day.

What you recall now is being just one in a sea of heads, bodies pressed and jostling, pushed forward because it was the only place anyone could go. You felt heat, smelled sweat, waded through a potpourri of pheromones so thick you could taste them on the back of your tongue.

The crowd moved with such inevitable momentum that you could lift your feet and travel with it beyond your input. Never had you felt more like an atom, a mere component of solid matter, significant only in theory, discernable only in grouping.

Steeping in that memory now, you begin to wonder if you have any individuality at all. If you aren't just surrendering at every moment to stronger forces, be they corporate, capitalist, social or cosmic.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of Childhood
strDesc:
The memory of being a kid again arrives to you without warning, and it comes as quite a shock. After all this time fending for yourself, squeezing every dollar you can out of the corporate grind, you had almost forgotten there was a time when you needed someone else to survive.

But you did. Everyone around here did. That's why the corps built the work houses, the group homes, the gymnasium-sized neonatal intensive care units. And with every new such gesture they demand gratitude, despite the fact that everyone knows exactly what they are doing: protecting their assets.

The person you remember now was the last to protect you, to show you real kindness, to look at you as if you weren't an investment but a person, with hopes and dreams and fears about tomorrow.

Standing at their funeral you had a sinking feeling that nothing would ever be the same. After a few words their body was summarily spirited away for processing, yet another asset for the corp, and you were left alone.

You've been fending for yourself ever since.

You do the difficult work of processing the harsh memory. While it causes you immediate emotional pain, it moves you closer to resolving the memory and its attendant traumas permanently. This kind of work, you're certain, will eventually yield deep emotional rewards.

strTitle: Clobbered by a Memory of Witnessing Violence
strDesc:
The memory, if it can even be called that, appears as a gap, a vacuum, a lacuna of remembering that flashes sights and sounds disconnected from any sense of narrative. Here is the blood, then nothing. There is the severing, the smell of metal and salt, then nothing again. Accompanying each flash are the noises: the screams, the guttural moans, the mewling pleas for help that will never arrive.

The terror that grips you now grows from a particular certainty: the body is fragile, leaking, stuffed with vital machines that can fail in a mere moment. It is not the thought of death that scares you. That is an inevitability you faced long ago.

It is the pondering of how you will die that frightens you. In what manner will you go?

There is a cause of death in your future, and you're paralyzed by the question of what that cause will be.

strTitle: Process the Memory of Witnessing Violence
strDesc: As hard as it is to remember now, you're certain something did happen that day. The images of violence are too specific, too repeated, too stable to be mere flights of your imagination. The contorted faces especially give you pause: they are blurred, but somehow still familiar. It strikes you that looking back at the memory now feels like casting a line into a mist covered pond. Your lure arcs out and disappears into fog, leaving you to wonder if it ever struck the surface at all. But as you wait you get nibbles. Little jerks on the rod, each one bringing the memory into greater focus. The blood, once filling the entire frame of your vision, now gathers and pools near the bodies. The screams, each once indistinguishable from the next, find timbre and pitch. The blurred faces, gradually, find form. So here you wait, slowly remembering, hoping the nibble might eventually become a bite. And while you know it will take time, there is cold comfort in the fact that this moment from your past is, while horrible, very real.

strTitle: Revisited by the Memory of Witnessing Violence
strDesc:
The catastrophic scene of violence returns to you. The blood, the howls, the stench of death. All the details appear as before in the same precise resolution.

But this time the memory arrives not as a havoc of images with no context but instead as a story. A sequence of events with cause and effect. A grim tale, no doubt, but one with a beginning, a middle, a terrible end.

They were alive, able bodied, intact. Then the violence fell, and their bodies were mangled. Then they lay bleeding. Some found their end.

And while you were lucky that day, you know one day that you'll find yours. It's an end you can flee from, or it is one you can ponder.

In either case that end will remain, a cause lurking in the dark corridor of your future, crouched like a predator ready to pounce. Whether the end is distant or near at hand is something you'll never know.

strTitle: Process the Revisitation of Your Memory of Witnessing Violence
strDesc:
You stare down the corridor ahead, squint into its blackness. Every moment is a step ahead. There is no turning back. Time only moves in one direction, after all.

It strikes you now that while each moment brings you closer to your end, each also presents within it a choice. And from that choice branches others, each then splitting into more choices, more moments, on and on in an infinite fractal of possible futures.

Time is not a corridor, then, merely because it leads to death. It is an ever stretching horizon, an endless flashing desert of moments, all of which feature a version of you within them.

Yes, you will die. But before that, you will live. What will you do in this moment? The only moment you are, at least in the very present, guaranteed?

Your memory of that violent day arrives to you again, now transformed. It appears neither as an unrecognizable sequence of images nor as an inevitable consequence of fate.

Instead, you see it in full clarity: a catastrophic moment that changed you, but does not have to define you. It is neither a zero, nor an open wound, but a scar.

And what now of your future? How will you move forward, scarred as you are by the trauma of being so intimate with death?

strTitle: Resolve Your Memory of Witnessing Violence
strDesc:
On the Venusian aerostat of Encantado, a small culture of artisans have dedicated their life to preserving the ancient Japanese and Brazilian craftwork of their former homes.

One such craft is Kintsugi, the restoration of cracked and damaged ceramics using gold leaf not to hide the flaws of a broken object but instead to highlight them, to render the object complete once again, now with the history of its former trauma legible on its surface.

It strikes you now that you are one such vessel. Instead of cowering in fear of this violent moment from your past, you've embraced it. Rather than hiding the scars of that day you have resolved to gild them, to integrate them fully, and beautifully, with your present self.

With every passing moment we accrue the physical artifacts of time. Dust. Scars. Wrinkles near our eyes, eyes which eventually cloud over and one day close forever.

But these artifacts are the stuff living. The patina of life, now a protest against death. A physical testament to your past and your presence, however brief, in this System.

You've managed with long internal struggle to fully process your memory of violence. Through this, you've gained a new and rare optimism. It will serve you well in the future.

They haul you before an AyoSec magistrate for judgement, leaving your ship and gear behind.

At the hearing, you can beg for just a fine- $15,000 payable in full or installments- or it's off to Azikiwe Brig for a year or three of hard labor.

It's almost like they want you to break the rules so they can extract more fines from you.

You sign reams of paperwork in tight cuffs.

It's a prison sentence of another sort. Another weight hanging around your neck.

New $15,000 fine added.

strDesc:

It's a prison sentence of another sort. Another weight hanging around your neck.

After processing, you are unceremoniously dumped outside the OKLG Security Office. If you weren't docked at station when you were arrested, you may need to use the PASS ferry service (on your PDA) to reach your ship again. Hopefully it's still there.

Time to get back on your feet.
You are hauled off to Azikiwe Brig.

You spend months laboring without wage among outcasts both meek and dangerous. You share a dorm with nearly a hundred other souls, all of you stacked like kindling on triple high bunks.

But even a hell like this is not without opportunity. You hear things. You meet people. You get in a few scrapes, you find a few friends. By the end of your term you've lived a dozen lives and you have all the scars to prove it.

Plus, free room and board. In a certain light, it might all be worth it ...

It's a prison sentence of another sort. Another weight hanging around your neck.

New $15,000 debt added, with payment due each shift.

After all your work cracking the Camellia Middlemist case, you and the DA might as well be close personal friends. That is made very clear during the pre-trial hearing, when a Very Important Somebody approaches the magistrate during your plea.

 You're let off the hook without another word. Nothing better than having friends in high places.

After processing, you are unceremoniously dumped outside the Venusian Security Office. If you weren't docked at station when you were arrested, you may need to use the PASS ferry service (on your PDA) to reach your ship again. Hopefully it's still there.

Time to get back on your feet.

After processing, you are escorted outside the NCPD Security Office. If you weren't docked at station when you were arrested, you may need to use the PASS ferry service (on your PDA) to reach your ship again. Hopefully it's still there.

Time to get back on your feet.
You are hauled off to NewCal Brig.

You spend months laboring without wage among outcasts both meek and dangerous. You share a dorm with nearly a hundred other souls, all of you stacked like kindling on triple high bunks.

But even a hell like this is not without opportunity. You hear things. You meet people. You get in a few scrapes, you find a few friends. By the end of your term you've lived a dozen lives and you have all the scars to prove it.

Plus, free room and board. In a certain light, it might all be worth it...

After processing, you are unceremoniously dumped outside the NewCal Security Office. If you weren't docked at station when you were arrested, you may need to use the PASS ferry service (on your PDA) to reach your ship again. Hopefully it's still there.

Time to get back on your feet.

strTooltip: Pay for first aid or treatments that can remove chronic ailments.

strDesc:
You know this is more like a placebo or bandaid compared to the state-of-the-art treatments rich folks enjoy on Hangzhou or Mars.

It's painful, probably not sterile, and beyond the budgets of most spacers, but it's the only option around for helping your space-wracked body become a little less of a mess.

Cost: $2,500

Treat injuries with a nanorobotic injection that stimulates wound healing and immune response to infection and some diseases for a handful of hours.

(Only options you can afford are available)
You sign away your right to protest both the exorbitant prices and any potential side effects, then grit your teeth and suffer through the necessary procedures.
